narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your LSAT score of 40, which is above the typical 36.7 average, and your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68% probability that you would fail it—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower end compared to peers who typically pass the bar. This outweighed your stronger Year 1 decile of 5, which is closer to the average of 5.7 and nudged things slightly toward success, but not enough to offset the later dip. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, a bit under the 3.2 average, played a supporting role but wasn't among the top influences here. Meanwhile, your above-average LSAT was the strongest push toward predicting a pass, showing the model recognized that strength as a key asset for bar success. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having competitive test scores and full-time commitment, but the sharp drop in your Year 3 grades made the model view you as higher risk than most admits, who maintain steadier performance across years. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income home—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent grades through tutoring or study habits, retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and consider part-time programs or gap-year experience to demonstrate upward trends. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps with a letter explaining your Year 3 challenges and recovery plan, as schools value resilience. You're clearly capable, and with these steps, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
